title: 第 5 節
en: When the victim, whether a horse or a bull, was to be killed, the priests knelt down and said : '0 Bull, our father ! be not angry with us and do not complain ! Die for us, for the glory of the gods and for our welfare ! If thou die, we shall live.' This is supposed to be the survival of a human sacrifice, and there are certainly some traces left of human victims having been immolated in former times. But even apart from that the custom of asking forgiveness of the animals before they were killed was very general. The following is a prayer of women addressed to Angue-Patay : ' Give us benches full of children, and let them be in perfect health ! We ought to immolate two women in thy name, and we kill only two sheep.' How old these sacrifices and the hymns which the Mordvinians recited to Mainof must have been, we may gather from the fact that the people confessed they did no longer understand them. ' Ce sont de bonnes paroles,' they said, 'mais nous ne savons plus ce qu'elles veulent dire' (p. 37). Might not the Brahmans even during the Brahma/ia period have said the same ? Some of the Vl] S0CIETE FINNOOUGRIENNE. 47 1 invocations are only whispered (upamsu, like some of the nivids of the Ya^ur-veda), such as (p. 3 7) : —
' Chim-Paz, Sun-god, ' Velen-Paz, God of the ' Vany-Paz, guardian-god, fields, 1 Mastorom-Paz ', Earth-god, ' Vanymizton, save us ! '

' Vanymizton, save us ! ' ' Anguepatiai'-Paz, Goddess ' Nichke-Paz, Great god, mother, ' Svet Vernichke, blood-red ' Oznymizton, pray for us ! ' light, We see, therefore, among the half-civilised FinnoUgrian tribes many residua, preserved almost to the present day, of a system of sacrifices intelligible in their origin, but in their present form often merely traditional, artificial, complicated, and unintelligible. We see that prayers addressed to the deities of nature were perfectly possible without sacrificial gifts, while sacrificial gifts without prayers, or without some words addressed to those for whom they were intended, are nowhere to be met with. A mute sacrifice is no sacrifice at all, a prayer unaccompanied by any sacrificial demonstrations is perhaps the best, certainly the most natural, and therefore probably the earliest manifestation of religious sentiment.

If, therefore, we may learn some lessons from a comparative study of other religions, and if even Veclic poetry and Vedic sacrifices are not so entirely different from the poetry and the ceremonial of other religions as to withdraw themselves from comparison, we may in future treat the religious poetry of the Veda as antecedent to the ceremonial of the Brahma/ias, though fully admitting that the priests who employed these hymns, even after they had lost 1 Also Mastor-Paz, p. 46, and Mastyr-Paz.
472 S0CTETE FINNO-OUGRIENNE. [chap.
a perfect understanding of them, modified them, corrupted them, and imitated them without any misgivings.

We owe much to the Brahmawa priests. To them, and more particularly to the HotW priests, who had to learn the whole collection of the hymns by heart in order to recite them at the great sacrifices, we probably owe the almost miraculous preservation of Vedic poetry, but certainly not its first creation. We must be grateful to the sacrificial age, the age of the Brahma^as, for having preserved by means of a most perfectly organised oral tradition whatever was left at their time of genuine ancient poetry, just as the Mordvines are grateful to Kouzma for having collected a kind of prayer-book and sacrificial guide for their priesthood.
"Vedic Deities.
It has sometimes been maintained that whatever the origin of the Vedic gods may have been, the poets of the Veda knew nothing about it, as little as Homer knew the origin of Apollon or Athene.
Yaska's Classification of Vedic Devatas.

But when we consult Yaska, one of the earliest Indian theologians, who lived before Pacini, and probably before the rise of Buddhism, we find him not only fully aware of the physical character of the Vedic gods, but classifying them at once according to the various places and spheres of activity which each of them occupied in nature. He divides them into three classes, gods of the earth, gods of the air, and gods of the sky.
VlJ DEVATA. 473 Devata.

In examining Yaska's ancient catalogue of the gods, we must bear in mind that with him deity or devata means something different from what it means with us. Every object that is praised by a poet in a hymn or even in a single verse, is in his technical language a devata, a deity. We should simply say it was the object of worship or praise. Thus, if a horse is praised, or a bird, or frogs, or a stone, a chariot, a bow, a bowstring, a whip, or a mortar, they are all put down as devatas. I wonder that this has not been used as a proof of the existence of fetish-worship among the Vedic Rishis. Would not the existence of stones, chariots, bows, &c, as Devatas seem to settle once for all the existence of fetish-worship in the Veda ? It would certainly be a stronger argument than many that have been produced for that purpose, though we see that the name of devata, deity, owes its origin simply to a theory of a theological school, and meant with them no more than an object praised, magnified, or sanctified.
The Three Classes of Vedic Deities.

In the arrangement which Yaska gives of the principal deities of the Veda, it is not always easy to distinguish between ancient tradition and later theory. If I say later theory, I mean, of course, later in comparison with the Vedic hymns and the Brahmanas. We must always remember that what we call the Nirukta, which is ascribed to Yaska, presupposes the Nighanto or the Nighan^us, the lists of words of which the Nirukta forms but a kind of commentary.
474 THE THREE CLASSES OF VEDIC DEITIES. [chap.

Now these Nighan^us are certainly ancient ; they are actually mentioned in an important passage of the Dipavansa, V, 62. Here the young Tissa is introduced as asking the Thera Siggava a difficult question concerning the Big-veda, the Ya(/ur-veda, the Samaveda, and also the Nighawfu (not yet the Nirukta), and fifthly the Itihasa. This is supposed to have taken place in 359 B.C. It is true that the Dlpavawsa cannot be older than the beginning of the fourth century a.d., but it rests on older authorities, particularly on the Att/takatha of the Mahavira fraternity ; and the absence of any mention of the Atharva-veda would seem to assign to the statement in question a very considerable antiquity. Anyhow we see from this passage the high authority assigned to the Nighawfci even by the early Buddhists, and we may gather from it at all events that the Nigha>iftis were pre-Buddhistic.

Yaska, the author of the Nirukta, quotes representatives of his own theories, the so-called Nairuktas, who had lived before his time, and if Yaska is older than Parani, this would bring us to at least 500 B.C. But many of these Nairukta theories are based on passages in the Brahma/ias, nay even in the hymns, and would therefore prove a still more remote antiquity for the period in which theological speculation, as represented in the Brahmanas, was rife in India. It can also be proved by independent evidence that the fundamental theory on which Yaska's division of the Vedic deities is based, namely the three localities to which the principal deities are referred, dates certainly from the Brahmawa period, nay has, as we shall see, certain warrants even in the hymns.
Vl] TRIAD OF VEDIC DEITIES. 475 Triad of Vedic Deities.

Apart from the philosophical doctrine that all gods are only manifestations of the supreme Self, the Atman, Yaska quotes, as we saw, the Nairuktas in support of a triad of gods, (i) those of the earth, (2) those of the air, and (3) those of the sky. Agni (fire), as Yaska says, has his place on earth, Vayu (wind) or Indra in the air, and Surya (sun) in the sky.
Number of Gods.

This triad of deities is not Yaska' s invention. It is clearly indicated already in the Brahmanas. Thus we read in the Aitareya-brahmana that Pra^apati created three greatnesses, Agni, Vayu, Surya. In the iiMndogya-Upanishad (IV, 17, 1) we read: ' Pra(/apati brooded over the worlds, and from them thus brooded over he squeezed out the essences, Agni from the earth, Vayu from the sky, Aditya (the sun) from heaven.'

Even in the hymns this threefold division of earth, air, and sky, or, as sometimes translated, earth, sky, and heaven, is well established. Thus we read in Rv. X, 65, 9, of terrestrial gods, parthiva, of celestial, divya, and of those who dwell in the waters (clouds), ye apsu. Their number is given as thirty-three (Rv. I, 45, 1 ; III, 6, 9; VIII, 28, 1), divided into three classes of eleven each.
The Thirty-three Gods.
This number of thirty-three must be old, for it occurs in the Avesta also. According to another division, however, the gods were not thrice eleven, vol. 11. E 476 THE THIETY-THEEE GODS. [cHAP.

but were twelve Aditvas. eleven Rudras. and eight Yasus. The names of these three classes occur in the hymns (I, 45, 1), but not their respective numbers, except that of the Aditvas. which in the hymns is given as seven, not yet as twelve. In the /Satapatha-brahma/~<a IV. 5.7. 2, their numbers are given, and Dyaus and PWthivi, or Indra and Pra(/apati, are added in order to bring the number up to thirty-three. A similar account is given in the Aitareya-brahma/<a II, 18, where the two additional gods are Pra^rapati and Yashafkara. There are other classifications of these gods in the hymns of the Pig-veda, such as : —
II, 3. 4. Yasus. Yisve Devas. Adityas, III, 20, 5, I, 45, 1, Yasus, Eudras, Adityas, IY, 8, 8, Adityas, Eudras, Yasus, YII. 51, 3, Adityas. Maruts, Devas, X, 25. 1, Eudras, Yasus, Visve.

Sometimes the number of the gods seems to be raised at random, though the number three prevails throughout. Thus in Pv. IY, 9, 9, we read of 3,339 gods who worshipped Agni, while there is a curious passage in the B?^'had-ara/iyaka-Upanishad III, 9, where the number of the gods is first stated as 3,306, and then, step by step, reduced to thirty- three, to six, to three, to two, to one-and-a-half, and finally to one.

According to Yaska the three principal deities dwelling on earth, in the air, and in the sky, i.e. Agni, Indra, and Aditya, receive different names according to their different activities which are celebrated by the Yedic poets. They are conceived also as endowed with various forms. The Supreme Self (Atman) at the root of all the gods is, of course, Vl] THE THIRTY-THREE GODS. 477 without any form whatever, but the individual deities are supposed to be endowed with form. nay. in many cases with human form. In the case of deities like Fire, Wind, and Sun, the form is indicated by the name ; in the case of deities like fVatavedas, Rudra, Indra, Pan/anya, and Asvinau, who do not exhibit so clearly the visible objects in nature from which they sprang, it is clear at least that they are praised as if they were not only sentient, but intelligent also, and capable of understanding what is said to them and of them. They are conceived, in fact, as manlike, and as possessing the ordinary members of human beings. The Yedic poets mention, for instance, the arms of Indra (IV, 31, 3), the fist of Indra (III, 1,5); they also ascribe to them such things as belong to men only. Thus Indra is said to have a beautiful wife (III, 20, 1), and his two horses are the Haris, 2, 4, 6, 8, or 10 in number. Vayu (wind), too, has his Xiyuts (steeds), Surya (sun) his Harits, Pushan (sun) his A^/as (goats), Ushas (dawn) her Arunis (reddish horses). The acts, again, which they are said to perform are like the acts of men. They hear and see, they eat and drink, and this not only like animals, but like men who understand, and are conscious of what they are doing.

There have, however, been differences of opinion on this point. Some ancient interpreters of the Veda seem to have argued that the gods, such as Fire, ^Yind, Sun, Earth, and Moon, were not endowed with a human form, and that their being addressed as if they were intelligent beings, proves nothing, because rivers, plants, dice, and other things also are addressed in the same way, as if E 2 478 THE THIRTY-THREE GODS. [chap.
zh: 當祭祀的犧牲品，無論是馬或牛，要被殺死時，祭司們跪下說：「噢牛啊，我們的父親！不要對我們生氣，也不要抱怨！為我們而死，為了眾神的榮耀與我們的福祉而死！若你死去，我們將活著。」據說這是人類祭祀的遺存，確實也留下了一些痕跡，表明在過去曾有過人類被當作祭品獻祭。但即使撇開這一點不談，在宰殺動物之前向它們求饒的習俗也是極其普遍的。以下是婦女向安古埃–帕泰（Angue-Patay）獻上的一篇禱詞：「賜予我們滿滿的兒女之凳，願他們都健康無恙！我們應當以你的名義獻祭兩位婦女，然而我們僅殺死兩隻羊。」這些祭祀及莫德維尼亞人向邁諾夫（Mainof）吟誦的讚美詩有多古老，我們可從一個事實推測：該民族承認他們已不再理解這些話語的含意。他們說：「Ce sont de bonnes paroles, mais nous ne savons plus ce qu'elles veulent dire」（第37頁）。難道梵天期間的婆羅門人也不曾說過同樣的話嗎？有些祈禱文僅以低聲耳語的方式誦讀（upamsu，如同雅祖吠陀〔Yajur-veda〕中的某些尼維德〔nivids〕），例如（第37頁）：—
「奇姆-帕茲，太陽神」、「維倫-帕茲，田野之神」、「瓦尼-帕茲，守護神」、「馬斯托羅姆-帕茲，大地神」、「瓦尼米茲同，拯救我們！」

「凡尼米茲頓，拯救我們！」「安古帕蒂艾-帕茲，女神」「尼赫克-帕茲，偉大的神、母親、」「斯維特‧維爾尼赫克，血紅之」「奧茲尼米茲頓，為我們祈禱！」光，我們看見，因此在半開化的芬烏戈爾民族中，我們看到許多殘跡，幾乎保存至今日，屬於一套祭祀制度，其起源仍可理解，但其現今形式往往不過是傳統的、人為的、複雜的，且不可理解的。我們看到對自然諸神祇的祈禱完全可以不須祭祀供物而進行，然而沒有祈禱、或沒有對其預定接收者所說的某些言語的祭祀供物，則無處可見。無言的祭祀根本不成其為祭祀，不伴隨任何祭祀示現的祈禱也許是最好的，肯定是最自然的，因此很可能是宗教情感最早期的表現。
如果我們能夠從比較研究他種宗教中汲取若干教訓，而且縱使吠陀詩歌與吠陀祭祀並非全然有異於他種宗教的詩歌與儀式以至於無法進行比較，那麼在今後我們可以將吠陀的宗教詩歌視為婆羅門書（Brāhmaṇa）的儀式的先行者，儘管我們充分承認，那些運用這些讚歌的祭司，甚至在他們已經喪失¹ --- ¹ 亦見《Mastor-Paz》，第 46 頁，及《Mastyr-Paz》。
472 芬烏戈里亞學會。[第幾章]
對於它們有著完美的理解，修改了它們、腐蝕了它們，並且毫無疑慮地模仿了它們。
我們對婆羅門祭司欠下許多。特別是對何塔祭司（Hotṛ），他們必須將整個讚歌集全部背誦下來，以便在盛大祭祀中吟誦，我們很可能要歸功於他們，才有了吠陀詩歌幾近奇蹟般的保存——儘管絕非其最初的創作者。我們應當感謝祭祀時代、婆羅門書時代，藉由組織最完善的口頭傳統方式，將那個時代遺存的所有真正古代詩歌保存下來，就如同莫爾德瓦人感謝庫茲馬（Kouzma）為他們的祭司編纂了某種祈禱書與祭祀指南一樣。

在吠陀時代，我們發現宗教思想有一種特殊的形式，此後在人類歷史中不曾再出現過。通過比較神話學與比較神學的研究，我們得以認識到一種宗教階段，此階段既不屬於純然的多神教（polytheism），亦不屬於純然的一神教（monotheism），而是介乎兩者之間。這種宗教形式稱為**單一神教**（henotheism）——此詞乃由我所造——其意謂著信徒在任何特定時刻，皆可專注於某一位神祇，並將之視為至高無上者，儘管同時仍然信奉其他眾神的存在。 在梨俱吠陀（Rig-Veda）中，我們目睹了這一過程的運作。每一首讚美詩皆獻給某位特定的提婆，而詩人在讚頌該位神祇時，往往賦予其宇宙的最高地位。例如，當吾人諷誦讚頌特尤斯（Dyaus）的詩篇時，特尤斯被描繪為至高無上的父神；然而當吾人轉向讚頌因陀羅（Indra）或阿耆尼（Agni）時，這些神祇依次被賦予同樣至高無上的地位。此一現象既非多神教之無原則的雜亂，亦非一神教之嚴格的單一性，而是代表了一個獨特的宗教與思想階段。
有時有人主張，無論吠陀諸神的起源如何，吠陀的詩人對此一無所知，就如同荷馬對阿波羅（Apollon）或雅典娜（Athene）的起源同樣一無所知。
雅斯卡對吠陀提婆的分類
但當我們查閱耶斯卡（Yaska），這位最早的印度神學家之一，他生活於帕尼尼之前，很可能也在佛教興起之前，我們發現他不僅充分認識到吠陀諸神的物質性格，而且立即根據每位神祇在自然界中所佔據的各種位置與活動領域對它們進行分類。他將諸神分為三類：地球之神、空氣之神和天空之神。

提婆塔（Devata）一詞在梵文中的字面意義為「閃耀者」（the Shiner），源自動詞 *div*，意為「閃耀」（to shine）。在吠陀時代，此詞用以指稱諸神，即那些以光明形式顯現於人類想像中的各種自然力量。提婆塔代表了對自然現象的神聖化理解，將風、火、水、太陽等自然力量人格化為具有神聖品質的神靈。 在比較神話學與宗教學的研究中，提婆塔的概念具有重要意義。它展示了雅利安宗教思想如何通過隱喻與想像，將抽象的自然力量轉化為具體的神聖人格。提婆塔並非如一神教中的至高唯一之神，而是多神教框架下眾多神靈中的一種分類方式，每一提婆塔都代表某一特定的自然現象或宇宙力量。 > 提婆塔的崇拜反映了人類對自然界的原始宗教回應，以及語言在建構神聖意義中所扮演的關鍵角色。

在考察耶斯卡（Yaska）的古代神祇目錄時，我們必須銘記於心，對他而言，神性或提婆（devata）的含義與對我們的含義大不相同。凡是詩人在讚美詩或甚至單一詩句中所讚美的任何對象，在他的技術用語中都是一位提婆，即一位神祇。我們簡單地說，那是被崇拜或讚美的對象。因此，如果馬、鳥、青蛙、石頭、戰車、弓、弦、鞭子或臼被讚美，它們都被列為提婆。我驚奇於此事為何未被用作吠陀聖賢（Rishis）中存在物靈崇拜的證據。難道石頭、戰車、弓等作為提婆的存在，不會一勞永逸地確立物靈崇拜在吠陀中的存在嗎？這肯定是比許多為此目的所提出的論證更強有力的論據，儘管我們看到提婆、神祇這個名稱的起源僅僅源於某一神學學派的理論，對他們而言不過意味著一個被讚美、被歌頌或被聖化的對象。
吠陀提婆的三個等級
在耶斯卡（Yaska）對吠陀主要提婆的分類中，並不總是容易區分古代傳統與後世理論。當我說後世理論時，當然是指相比於吠陀讚歌與梵書（Brahmanas）而言的較晚時期。我們必須時刻記住，我們所謂的《尼魯克塔》（Nirukta），雖歸屬於耶斯卡，卻是預設了《尼岡圖》（Nighantu/Nighantus）——即詞彙表——而《尼魯克塔》不過是對該詞彙表的一種註疏形式。
474 吠陀提婆的三個等級 [章節]

現在這些尼笈多（Nighan^us）確實是古老的；它們在《島史》（Dipavansa）第五卷第62節的一個重要段落中有確實的記載。這裡年輕的帝沙被引入向長老西嘎瓦提出一個關於梨俱吠陀、夜柔吠陀、娑摩吠陀，以及尼笈多（當時還不是《根本經》）的難題，第五項則是歷史傳記。這據說發生在西元前359年。誠然《島史》的成書時間不會早於西元四世紀初，但它基於更古老的權威著作，特別是大雄派的《阿闍婆註疏》（Atthakattha）；而《阿闍婆吠陀》完全缺乏提及，這似乎表明所述的這一記載具有相當古遠的年代。無論如何，我們從這段記載可以看出，尼笈多在早期佛教徒中被賦予了極高的權威性，而且我們至少可以從中推斷尼笈多是前佛教時期的產物。

耶斯卡（Yaska）乃《尼盧克塔》（Nirukta）之作者，他引述了自己理論的代表人物，即所謂的尼盧克塔派（Nairuktas），這些人物生活在他之前的時代；若耶斯卡早於帕拉尼（Parani），這將使我們至少回溯到公元前五百年。然而，這些尼盧克塔派的理論中，許多是以梵書（Brahmanas）中的段落，甚至讚歌本身的段落為基礎，因此會證明以梵書所代表的神學思辨在印度盛行的時期具有更為久遠的年代。藉由獨立的證據，亦可證明耶斯卡對吠陀諸提婆之區分所基於的基本理論——即主要提婆被歸屬於三個地域的理論——無疑源自梵書時期，且如我們後來所見，甚至在讚歌中亦有一定的根據。

吠陀宗教中最值得注意的特色之一，便是提婆（Deva）的三神組。在最古老的吠陀讚歌中，我們屢次發現有三位主要的神祇：特尤斯（Dyaus）、因陀羅（Indra）與阿耆尼（Agni）。 這三位神祇各自代表了不同的自然力量與宗教原則。特尤斯象徵天空與光明，因陀羅掌管風雨與戰爭，而阿耆尼則為火焰與祭祀的神明。在吠陀信仰中，這三神組合成了一個和諧的體系，反映出古代雅利安人對宇宙秩序的理解。 值得留意的是，這種三神組的觀念並非孤立的現象，而是與更廣泛的宗教思想相連繫。它揭示了從多神教（polytheism）向單一神教（henotheism）發展的過程——在特定的儀式或崇拜情境中，一位神祇會被頌揚為至高者，而其他神祇則退居次要地位。 因此，研究吠陀提婆的三神組，乃是理解印度宗教早期階段及其後來發展的關鍵所在。
除卻認為一切諸神皆為至高自性、梵我（Atman）之顯現的哲學主張外，雅斯卡如我們所見，引用了尼盧克塔派（Nairuktas）的觀點，以支持三元神聖論：（一）地界之神，（二）空界之神，（三）天界之神。雅斯卡指出，阿耆尼（Agni，火神）居於地界，伐由（Vayu，風神）或因陀羅（Indra）居於空界，蘇利耶（Surya，日神）居於天界。
神祇的數量

這三位提婆並非耶斯卡（Yaska）的創造。在《梵書》中早已明確指出。因此我們在《愛達雷耶梵書》中讀到，梵天（Prajāpati）創造了三種偉大的力量：阿格尼（Agni）、瓦尤（Vayu）、蘇利耶（Surya）。在《查多格耶奧義書》（Chāndogya-Upanishad）（四‧一七‧一）中我們讀到：「梵天對諸世界進行沉思冥想，因此從他的沉思冥想中他擠出精華，從大地擠出阿格尼，從虛空擠出瓦尤，從天堂擠出阿迪提耶（Aditya）（太陽）。」
即使在讚歌中，大地、空氣與天空這三重劃分，或如有時譯法所言的大地、天空與天界，也已確立無疑。如此我們在《梨俱吠陀》X, 65, 9 中讀到地上的提婆（parthiva）、天上的提婆（divya），以及居住於水域（雲層）中的提婆（ye apsu）。它們的數目定為三十三（《梨俱吠陀》I, 45, 1；III, 6, 9；VIII, 28, 1），分為三個類別，各十一個。

在梨俱吠陀中，我們反覆遇見「三十三位神祇」（*trayastrinçat devâh*）的提及。這個數字的來源及其含義，在印度註疏家之間引發了多番討論。最常見的解釋是將三十三分為三組：十一位天神（*adityas*），八位風神（*vasus*），十二位火神（*rudras*）。但這種分類方式並非始終一致，不同的韋達傳統對此有所變異。 有些學者認為，這三十三位神祇源自對自然現象的觀察——特別是太陽在其年週期中經歷的三十三個不同階段或轉變。也有人主張，這個數字乃是對天體的一種古老計數法，其具體含義在我們現今已不復可得。 然而，更深層的宗教意義在於，三十三這個數字似乎象徵著從多神教（*polytheism*）向更高形式的神聖觀念演化——即朝向對一位至高主神的認識，儘管祂以許多名號與化身而為人知。在這種理解之下，許多神祇最終可被視為單一無限者（the Infinite）的不同表現或面向。
這個三十三的數字必定很古老，因為它也出現在《阿維斯陀》中。然而，根據另一種分類方式，眾神並非三個十一。

但卻是十二位阿迪提耶（Adityas）、十一位魯陀羅（Rudras）和八位瓦蘇（Vasus）。這三類的名字出現在讚歌中（I, 45, 1），但未各自說明其數字，除了阿迪提耶外——在讚歌中它被給定為七個，而非十二個。在《百路梵書》（Satapatha-brahma）IV. 5. 7. 2 中，它們的數字被給定，並加上特尤斯和普里希維（Dyaus and Pṛthivi），或因陀羅和祈禱主（Indra and Prajāpati），以使數字達到三十三。類似的說法也出現在《愛達婦耶梵書》（Aitareya-brahma）II, 18 中，其中兩位額外的神祇是祈禱主和雅沙卡拉（Prajāpati and Yajñakara）。在《梨俱吠陀》的讚歌中存在著這些神祇的其他分類，例如：—
II, 3. 4. 雅蘇斯。毘什瓦‧提婆。阿迪提亞斯，III, 20, 5, I, 45, 1，雅蘇斯、烏德拉斯、阿迪提亞斯，IY, 8, 8，阿迪提亞斯、烏德拉斯、雅蘇斯，YII. 51, 3，阿迪提亞斯。瑪魯斯、提婆、X, 25. 1，烏德拉斯、雅蘇斯、毘什瓦。
有時眾神的數目似乎是隨意設定的，儘管三這個數字始終佔據主導地位。因此在《梨俱吠陀》四卷九頌九節中，我們讀到有三千三百三十九位神祇敬拜阿耆尼（Agni），而在《婆羅多奧義書》（B?^'had-ara/iyaka-Upanishad）第三卷九節有一段好奇的記載，其中眾神的數目首先被列為三千三百零六，然後逐步遞減為三十三、六、三、二、一又二分之一，最後歸結為一。

根據耶斯卡（Yaska）的說法，住居於地上、虛空中和天空的三位主要提婆——即阿格尼（Agni）、因陀羅（Indra）和阿迪提耶（Aditya）——根據吠陀詩人所讚頌的他們各自不同的活動而獲得不同的名稱。他們也被設想為具有各種形態。根植於所有提婆的至高自我（Atman）當然是無形的，但個別的提婆被認為具有形態，甚至在許多情況下具有人的形態。在火、風、太陽等提婆的情況下，形態由其名稱所指示；在瓦他毗陀斯（Vatavedas）、魯陀羅（Rudra）、因陀羅、帕爾尼耶（Parjanya）和阿濕溫雙神（Asvinavu）等提婆的情況下，他們並未如此清楚地展現出他們源出其中的自然可見對象，但至少明確的是，他們受到讚譚，彷彿他們不僅具有感知力，而且具有智慧，能夠理解向他們所說的話以及關於他們的言論。實際上，他們被設想為具有人的樣貌，並具有人類的普通肢體。例如，吠陀詩人提及因陀羅的臂膀（IV, 31, 3）、因陀羅的拳頭（III, 1, 5）；他們也將僅屬於人類的事物歸於他們。因此，據說因陀羅有一位美麗的妻子（III, 20, 1），他的兩匹馬是哈里斯（Haris），數量為 2、4、6、8 或 10 匹。瓦尤（Vayu，風）也有他的尼耶烏茲（Niyuts，駿馬），蘇利耶（Surya，太陽）有他的哈里茲（Harits），普山（Pushan，太陽）有他的阿什瓦斯（Asvas，山羊），烏莎斯（Ushas，晨曦）有她的阿魯尼斯（Arunis，紅棕色的馬）。再者，據說他們所執行的行為是人類行為的樣態。他們聽見和看見，他們飲食，這不僅如同動物一般，而是如同具有理解力的人類，並且意識到他們所做之事。
然而，對於這一點一直存在著見解的分歧。梨俱吠陀的一些古代詮釋者似乎主張，諸如火、風、太陽、大地和月亮這樣的提婆並非被賦予人形，而且它們被稱呼得彷彿是具有理智的存在，這並不能證明什麼，因為河流、植物、骰子和其他事物也同樣被以相同的方式稱呼，彷彿它們是有理智的。
